articles.title: Maharashtra Weather: The Wait for Rain Increases! Will the Monsoon Arrive by June 20? The Heatwave Continues in Mumbai - Lokshahi English News
articles.full_text:
Maharashtra Weather: The Wait for Rain Increases! Will the Monsoon Arrive by June 20? The Heatwave Continues in Mumbai
Mumbaikars are currently anxiously awaiting the arrival of the monsoon; despite the monsoon being active over the Arabian Sea, its progress in Maharashtra is delayed, leaving citizens without any relief from the heat. According to reports from the Indian Meteorological Department, a heatwave may develop in parts of Marathwada, central Maharashtra, and Vidarbha, while the Brihanmumbai region is also likely to experience hot and humid weather until June 16. During this period, the minimum temperature in Mumbai has been recorded at 30°C or higher over the past week, and the minimum temperature in the interior parts of the state is also above average; in some places, a difference of up to four degrees has been noted between maximum and average temperatures.
The recent daily weather reports have raised concerns among citizens and experts due to Maharashtra being suddenly excluded from the first phase of the monsoon arrival. Satellite images show that there are currently no rain clouds over Maharashtra, and meteorologists estimate that the monsoon could advance into the state between June 19 and 20. Experts have pointed to the conditions of anti-cyclonic winds and low humidity in the winds coming from the west as reasons; these winds are pushing hot air outward, hindering the formation of rain clouds.
According to forecasts from the regional weather department, hot and humid weather will persist in Mumbai, Thane, Palghar, and Raigad districts until June 16, and while there may be a possibility of rain with thunder after that, heavy rainfall is not expected. A heatwave warning has been issued, particularly for Akola, Amravati, Buldhana, and Washim in Marathwada and Vidarbha. Even after the monsoon has entered the Konkan and southern regions of western Maharashtra, the western advancement has stalled, with Pune likely to see maximum temperatures reaching 36°C; pre-monsoon rainfall signs are appearing in Kolhapur and the Ghats area. The arrival of the monsoon in Khandesh has been delayed, and daytime temperatures are expected to remain between 39°C and 42°C.
This weather anomaly has particularly increased risks for farmers, elderly citizens, and people working outdoors. Experts are currently monitoring humidity levels and advise the public to take precautions until a clearer picture of the rainy situation emerges. The weather department has suggested that the general public should avoid dehydration during the heat, refrain from going out during peak summer hours, and adhere to the instructions of local health and weather departments. Officials have also clarified that the government and local administration will activate heat-related health services and measures if necessary.
